text.content:
Starship Combat Sequence

Source: p. 61.

Starship combat runs in four segments: Piloting, Speed, First Fire, and Second and Subsequent Fire.

Piloting Segment. Pilots make evasive flying decisions and any piloting-based reactions. Evasion works like a reaction skill and adds the pilot's result, plus ship maneuverability, to incoming attack difficulty.

Speed Segment. Ships trying to close, run, or hold position resolve speed rolls here. Compare results against the opposing ship to see whether the range band changes.

First Fire Segment. Resolve the first volley from each attacking weapon crew. Gunnery uses the operator's starship gunnery skill plus the weapon's fire control.

Second and Subsequent Fire Segments. Any extra attacks continue here. Normal multiple-skill-use penalties apply to the character operating the ship or weapon, but ship codes themselves are not reduced by multiple actions.

Crew roles. Piloting, shields, and gunnery are separate jobs. One character can attempt several of them in a round, but the normal cumulative action penalties still apply to that character.

Speed and Pursuit

Source: p. 62.

Starship combat uses relative range bands, not personal-scale map distance. The practical bands are short, medium, and long.

When ships try to close or run, both sides make speed rolls using the ship's sublight speed code. Compare the results to determine whether range changes.

If the faster ship is trying to close and wins, the range band moves inward. If the faster ship is trying to escape and wins, the range band opens. A ship that successfully flees at long range leaves the battle area.

Holding position means fighting at the current range band instead of changing it.

Operational note. Speed rolls use ship code, not the pilot's skill. The pilot's main skill-based defensive action is evasion, which is resolved separately with starship piloting plus maneuverability.

Shield Attempts and Damage

Source: p. 63 and the charts page.

Shield attempts are declared against one incoming attack at a time. The operator rolls starship shields as a reaction skill. The shield difficulty depends on the attacker's range.

If the shield attempt succeeds, apply the shield result with the ship's hull when resolving the incoming damage. For torpedoes and missiles, a successful shield attempt dissipates the attack completely.

Damage resolution. Roll the attacking weapon's damage against the target ship's hull, applying shield protection when relevant. Medium-range hits reduce damage by -1D; long-range hits reduce damage by -2D.

Damage ladder. If the damage roll is lower than the ship's resistance, the ship is lightly damaged. If damage meets or exceeds resistance, the ship becomes heavily damaged. At double resistance it becomes severely damaged. Repeated escalation can lead to dead in space and then destroyed.

Common effects. The starship rules specifically call out ionized controls, blown shields, and reduced ship codes on heavier damage results. A severely damaged ship that is severely damaged again also rolls on the Starship System Damage Table.

Multiship Combat

Source: p. 64.

When several ships are in the same battle, track range separately by opponent. One ship may be at short range to one enemy, medium to another, and long to a third.

This is especially important in dogfights, pursuit scenes, and mixed battles where some ships are trading fire while others are breaking off or closing.

If a ship successfully flees at long range, it leaves the battle area instead of continuing to occupy the same encounter state.

GM use. Treat each opposing ship or dogfight as its own range relationship. Do not collapse all starships in the encounter into one shared range band unless the scene truly supports that simplification.

Astrogation

Source: pp. 58-60 and the astrogation chart on p. 142.

Standard trip. Find the route's standard duration, multiply it by the ship's hyperdrive multiplier, and roll astrogation against difficulty 15.

Time changes. Each extra day taken reduces difficulty by 1. Each day saved increases difficulty by 1. A hyperspace journey cannot be reduced below one day.

Nav computer limits. Without a nav computer, the normal standard-trip difficulty becomes 30. Small craft with stored or pre-calculated route data can still make trips if the needed coordinates are already carried.

Hasty entry. A combat-round hyperspace escape may be attempted by rolling each round until the astrogator succeeds. Once the jump is made, the trip's difficulty is doubled.

Ship damage modifiers. Light ship damage adds +5 to astrogation difficulty. Heavy ship damage adds +10.

Mishaps. Failed astrogation can trigger hyperdrive cut-out, radiation fluctuations, off-course arrival, mynocks, close calls, or collision, depending on the mishap roll.

Healing Quick Reference

Source: p. 53 and p. 142.

Medpac. Medicine may treat a wounded character once per combat round spent treating. Difficulties are 10 for Wounded, 15 for Incapacitated, and 20 for Mortally Wounded.

Medpac effect. A successful treatment reduces wound status by one degree: Mortally Wounded to Incapacitated, Incapacitated to Wounded, and Wounded to Healthy. A medpac is expended when used. Once a character is treated, medpacs do not continue reducing that same injury. If a Wounded character is restored to Healthy by medpac, at least one day must pass before medpac can heal a new wound on that character.

Rejuve tank. Healing is automatic. Roll 2D for the time needed: Wounded 2D hours, Incapacitated 2D days, Mortally Wounded 2D weeks.

Natural healing. Starting the day after injury, roll Strength once per day. Wounded: 2-6 Incapacitated, 7-11 no change, 12+ healed. Incapacitated: 2-8 dead, 9-13 no change, 14+ Wounded.
